practices.practice: Use a useEffect with the state in its dependency array to perform an action when that state updates.
Set state directly with the new value, e.g. setMovies(result), to replace the previous state value.
Use the functional updater form with spread syntax to merge new results into the previous array state, e.g. setMovies(prevMovies => [...prevMovies, ...result]).
Read the value in the render function instead of inside nested functions when you need the updated state.
Put the state variable in a useEffect dependency list and log or react to it in a separate effect.
conditions: use the react-hooks/exhaustive-deps eslint rule

practices.practice: Compute a temporary local variable and use that value immediately instead of reading state back.
Use a mutable ref to remember the latest value when you do not need a re-render.
conditions: only if you do not need state
only if you only want to remember the value

practices.practice: Treat state and props as immutable within a render and expect closures to keep the render's constant value.
Use the functional updater form inside the handler or async callback when updating from the current value.
Use the react-useStateRef / react-usestateref hook instead of React.useState so you can access the latest state through a ref while still rendering from state.
conditions: backward compatible with React.useState

practices.practice: Use the locally computed new value directly in your logic instead of reading the state back immediately after calling the setter.
Trigger a useEffect from the updated state by listing that state in the dependency array, and run the follow-up logic there.
Read the updated state on the next render instead of immediately after calling the setter.
Use useEffect with an empty dependency array to set state after the component mounts.
conditions: leave the dependency array blank

practices.practice: Use React.useRef() for an instant mutable value instead of waiting for state to update.
Rewrite the state management around useReducer instead of relying on useState setters for this shared application state.
Expose the reducer state and dispatch through React context so pages can read and update the shared state everywhere.
Create custom hooks that return a state slice together with a setter-like dispatch wrapper for that slice.

Initialize the state variable with a default value in the destructuring pattern, using `const [state = initialValue, setState] = useState()` instead of passing the initial value to `useState(initialValue)`.
Use `useState` with `useEffect` when you are concerned about using old state for a use case that needs that state.
conditions: when concerned with using old state for any use case

practices.practice: Use the push method to update state when you only need to update state.
Use a reload state value together with a useEffect that triggers downloadUrl1 when reload equals 4.
Run action on update of movies inside a useEffect that depends on [movies].
Make the effect callback async and pass the current movies value into a separate function called from onClick as onClick={() => myfunction(movies)}.
conditions: in the answer's async wrapper variant

practices.practice: Access the previous state and set a new updated value by using the functional updater form, such as appending `result` to the prior array with `setState(prev => [...prev, ...result])`.
conditions: when the state is an array

practices.practice: Use a custom hook like useAsyncWatcher to wait for state values to update and return the new value, optionally including the previous value.
conditions: promise wrapper around useEffect

practices.practice: Use a deep state hook like useAsyncDeepState whose setter can return a promise synchronized with the internal effect.
conditions: setter can be called with no arguments to subscribe to state updates

practices.practice: Capture the current state inside the functional updater callback instead of trying to read the setter result.
Run the alert inside a useEffect after the state update logic.
Compute a new object locally with the updated field (for example, let myDataNew = {...myData, content: x}) instead of reading state back immediately.
Create a custom hook that lets you pass a callback to the state setter so actions run after the state update completes.
conditions: functional components

practices.practice: Use a useEffect hook with the state variable in its dependency array so the callback runs after the state changes.
Wrap the state setter in a Promise that resolves from the setter callback so you can await the new state value.
Use the awaited value returned by that wrapper to update another piece of state after the setter runs.
Use useRef instead of state when you want to read and write a value immediately through .current.
Render the state value with JSON.stringify(movies) instead of returning the previous placeholder value.
Move console.log(movies) outside useEffect and place it right above the return so it runs in the render body and sees the updated state on the next render.
Create the entire array first and then set the state at the end of the loop.
See the new value on the next render instead of inside the event handler.
Add a useEffect hook so you can observe the value once it has been updated even though the setter is asynchronous.
Do not read the just-set state value and use the value you already have from your JSON instead.
Subscribe to the state variable using useEffect if you want to get new values of it.
Use a useEffect hook with squares in its dependency list so checkWinner runs after squares changes.
Use useEffect to respond to changes in state.
Use another effect to respond to those changes.
Use useEffect to handle the updated state instead of relying on the setter to update immediately.
Remove the useEffect and fetch the new value from the API on the rerender after setting inputsData to false on the button click.
Use a callback function as the state updater so the value updates accordingly.
Use a hook in a functional component so the component renders whenever the state changes.
Pass a callback function to the setter when you need to use the old value.
conditions: only when the new value depends on the old value

practices.practice: Set a definite value directly when it does not depend on the old value.
conditions: only when the new value does not depend on the old value

practices.practice: Read the value on the next render, such as in the body of the component before the JSX return.
Run the checks on the locally computed value when you write it to state instead of checking the stale instance afterward.
Move the checks into a useEffect that depends on the state constant so they run after the state changes.
Add another useEffect that listens for changes in the product state and sets isLoading to false after product has finished setting.
Use the updated value from the newly created function after React re-renders instead of relying on the old value captured in the current function.
Set the array state from the newly created array rather than from the old value captured in the current function.
Use useEffect to log or otherwise act on the value after it has changed.
Initialize the relevant state values to an empty string or empty array instead of leaving them undefined.
Use useRef instead of state if the quotes array is not shown in the UI and only needs to persist between renderings.
conditions: if the quotes array is not presented in the UI

practices.practice: Get the new value after the next render inside an effect.
Use useEffect to trigger the follow-up logic after the state has been updated.
Store the returned value from the prompt in a local variable and use that instead of trying to read the state immediately after calling the setter.
Use a useEffect hook to verify or observe the state after it has changed.
Remove the locally defined state variable and use the prop state directly instead of keeping two separate state variables.
Filter the array before setting it, instead of cloning it and then filtering and setting it.
Extract the filtering logic to a function and do the entire filtering in a single step.
Use a useEffect hook to listen to the state change instead of validating the value immediately after setting it.
Do not use the found state if it is unnecessary; use the description state directly instead.
Compute the derived values on each render instead of keeping them in their own state.
Use useMemo to avoid unnecessary recalculations when the computation is resource heavy or the component may render for unrelated changes.
conditions: if the computation is resource heavy
if the component might render because of unrelated changes

practices.practice: Memoize the component to avoid unnecessary calculations.
conditions: if the computation is resource heavy
if the component might render because of unrelated changes

practices.practice: Use the state in handlers and effects rather than expecting the setter to make it available immediately in the same render.
Use updater functions when you need to create new state from previous state or when accessing the state variable itself is inconvenient.
conditions: for multiple updates within the same event
when accessing the state variable itself is inconvenient

practices.practice: Use the functional update form of the useState setter when setting the same state variable multiple times so later updates do not overwrite earlier ones with stale values.
Use the functional updater form when pushing to a useState array so each update receives the previous array state.
Use the functional form of setState so each update to the array considers the current state at the time of the update.
Use the useEffect hook to run code after the state update has been applied.
Store the input value in state as the user changes it so the value is already available when submitting.
Add a callback to the state change and execute the API in that callback after the state has been changed.
Use the useCallback() hook with the state in its dependency array so the function executes on every state change.
Store the chess.js instance in a ref instead of creating a new one every time you want to make a move.
Inline the makeAMove logic and use the updated game value for the rest of the event handler instead of reading state back immediately.
Store the urls in a local array instead of relying on the useState value in the same render cycle.
Store the value in a temporary variable instead of reading state back immediately after calling the setter.
Put the logging or check in a useEffect that re-runs when the state changes so you can see the updated value.
conditions: without a dependency array

practices.practice: Use the change in randomNoteId to console.log the value and verify whether it really changed.
conditions: for testing purpose

practices.practice: Cache a new output in the function and call the hook at the end instead of calling setOptions inside the loop.
Create a useEffect that depends on the state so you can log or run code after the state updates.
Replace the state value directly instead of spreading the old prevData object.
Use useRef when you need unbound state to be reflected without waiting for a re-render.
conditions: for unbound state
without re-render

practices.practice: Add a useEffect to run logic after the state has been updated.
Use the selected value from the event directly instead of reading state back immediately after calling the setter.
Log event.target.value to verify the current selection instead of logging the state variable right after setState.
Use useEffect to access data that was recently updated after the state change has taken effect.
If you need the value immediately after calling the setter, use the same value you are passing to setCompanies or setServices instead of reading state back.
Do not await setState because setState does not return a promise.
Avoid keeping a separate "quotes" state variable if you can set the randomQuote value directly inside fetchData and display that value on the frontend.
Put the state variable in the useEffect dependency array if you need to do something with it after updating it.
Use the previous state in the setTodos function.
Pass the data directly from the React Hook Form values into the handleLogin request instead of relying on the user state.
Use a useEffect to log or otherwise access the latest state value after it has updated.
Use useEffect to perform actions after the state variable has been updated, with that state in the dependency array.
Pass a callback function to setState so React provides the previous state as an argument to that function.
Use a callback in the setSelectedData function so the update happens through that callback instead of reading the state immediately.
Log the state with useEffect or directly inside the JSX to confirm the updated value.
Use the fetched data directly in your JSX instead of reading it back from state inside the axios callback.
Read the updated state on the next render rather than expecting it immediately after dispatching or setting it.
Use the response object directly instead of reading the newly set state value immediately after calling the setter.
Create the new state separately in a local value if you need the data immediately instead of waiting for the next render.
Use a useEffect to check for the state change and call the validate function when that state changes.
Make the effect look for both the relevant state and the relevant handler/function so it can call the validate function.
Create a temporary source of truth from which both state and localStorage obtain their arguments.
Do not store feedback as state when it is derived from another state.
Use a state index to select the right question to display, similar to carousel functionality.
Watch the state value for changes using useEffect.
Do not create state for values that can be computed or derived from the input value; compute them instead.
Move the checkTypaheadMatches function into a useEffect hook.
Move the logic into a separate function and call that function after the state update path instead of expecting the setter to update synchronously.
Validate on any date change so the error message appears as soon as a selection is invalid.
Use the new json.nextPageToken directly in the URL construction instead of reading nextToken immediately after calling setNextToken.
Use the value from the event instead of the state when you need the actual value immediately.
Use the direct state setter rather than the alternative setter mentioned in the question.
Check the updated image state in a useEffect after calling setImage.
Preserve the existing state when setting it so a field like name does not overwrite the rest of the state.
Add the relevant piece of state to the dependency array if you insist on using the hook.
conditions: if you insist on using the hook

practices.practice: Use a ref hook so you can access the value anywhere.
conditions: if you insist on using the hook

practices.practice: Keep a separate simple JavaScript object for other computation while leaving React state alone for GUI rendering.
Use refs to hold values that need to persist across GUI re-renders.
Use state for values that need to be displayed in the GUI so React can track the changes and re-render when they change.
Use a boolean flag to guard against an async race condition so the later state update wins.
Use the functional updater form to merge the response data into the previous state object: setInputsData(oldState => ({...oldState, ...response.data})).
Have another useEffect with game_cards as the dependency so the alert runs every time game_cards changes.
Use a hook to watch for changes to the value after the callback updates it.
Use the functional updater form to serialize multiple state updates in the handler.
Add logging in a useEffect that listens for changes to authUser to verify the state update.
Use a useEffect with the relevant state in its dependency array to run code after the state update has completed.
Use a functional update so the callback always works with the latest snackbar state.
Remove the state value from the useCallback dependency array when the callback uses the functional updater.
Use a side effect with useEffect to run the log at the right moment after the state change.
Render the value in the component so you can see the updated state on a later render.
Use useEffect to do something whenever state changes.
Read the updated state during the next render, when React has re-rendered the component.
Use a useEffect hook if you only need the state when it changes rather than on every render.
Read the updated state on the next render, since the value is assigned in the next render after the fetch finishes.
conditions: during the fetch / after the communication with the server finishes

practices.practice: Put the logging or other follow-up logic in a useEffect that depends on the state so it runs whenever that state changes.
Use a useEffect to do something after the state has been updated.
Make a deep copy of the updated value before setting state so React gets a new reference.
Use the updated state on the next render rather than expecting it to change immediately.
Save the new state into a variable and use that variable in the callback instead of reading state back immediately.
Add some delay before calling the getData method.
Pass the needed values as parameters to the getData() method instead of reading state back later.
Do not keep totalPrice as its own state when it can be directly calculated from the cart; calculate it during rendering instead.
If the calculation is expensive, wrap it in useMemo so it only recalculates when cartItems change.
Use another useEffect if you want to run an operation right after the state change.
Use the spread operator to set an array state from the returned array data instead of dumping it directly into state.
conditions: when the backend returns an array

practices.practice: Console.log the state after the data is set rather than immediately after calling the setter.
Use callbacks to see the data after it is set.
Rely on the component's next render cycle to access the updated state instead of expecting it immediately after calling setState.
Use the updater function form of the state setter.
Log the value you are about to set instead of accessing the state variable immediately after calling the setter.
Calculate the new index manually and use it directly for both the state update and any other logic that depends on it.
Use a function with the previous value as its first argument when updating boolean or number state.
